analysis.text:
Labor productivity growth is running at 2.8 percent year over year, one quarterly reading inside the 2.7 to 3.4 band and well short of the two-consecutive-quarter break that would signal a genuine trend shift. One data point is not a pattern yet.

The jobs picture shows AI-exposed industries (information, professional/business, financial) contracting at -1.3 percent year over year while control industries (construction, leisure/hospitality, education/health) grow at 1.3 percent, a -2.5 point gap. That gap has sat inside its normal range for 21 straight months and is holding roughly flat, not widening, and it remains well short of the -4.8 alarm trigger. Wages tell a different story: exposed pay growth (4.6 percent) is actually running above control pay growth (3.5 percent), a positive 1.1 point differential, steady for nearly four years. That is hard to square with an active displacement story, since displaced-worker theory would expect exposed pay to lag, not lead. Job postings show a wide but stable -28 point spread between exposed and control occupations, essentially flat over six months and inside normal range.

Worker share of national income has now declined for four straight quarters to 95, though the payload frames the relevant test as acceleration away from long-run trend, which isn't something I can quantify here. The recent-graduate unemployment gap (4.7 points, three months running, above its ten-year average) is worth watching but is explicitly supplemental.

On causes: AI adoption is rising (20.6 percent of firms) and capability benchmarks and task-horizon measures continue to climb, so a deployment channel exists. But the yield curve is not inverted, real rates sit at 2.3 percent, and none of the exposed-sector alarms have tripped. The post-2021 tech-hiring correction and ordinary cyclical cooling remain fully consistent with what's in this data, especially given firming exposed-sector wages.

Overall read: the exposed sectors show a wide, stable jobs gap and firm relative wages, more consistent with cyclical or correction-driven softness than an accelerating AI-displacement break.
